reasoning: GaussianInt is defined as Zsqrtd (-1) with components re and im, so a Gaussian integer with im = 0 is precisely the image of an ordinary integer under ℤ → ℤ[i]. The Lean statement assumes a nonzero ideal I ≠ ⊥ and asserts the existence of z : I with z ≠ 0 and (z : GaussianInt).im = 0. Since z : I lives in the submodule subtype whose zero coerces to 0 in GaussianInt, z ≠ 0 correctly encodes "nonzero element in I", and the im = 0 clause correctly encodes "is an ordinary integer". This matches the informal claim that every nonzero ideal of the Gaussian integers contains a nonzero (rational) integer.
